reflection.quote: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
reflection.reading:
It's a Tuesday in late July. Nothing special. The kind of day that used to disappear entirely — either lost inside a hangover or bracing for the next one. Now it's just a day. Coffee that actually tastes like something. A run before it gets too hot. A text back to a friend I would have ghosted three years ago. The unremarkable stuff is the whole point.

When I was 22, I thought sobriety was a subtraction. Take away the drinking and you're left with a smaller, quieter, more boring version of your life. What I couldn't see was that the drinking wasn't adding anything — it was eating the days from the inside. The Tuesdays disappeared. Whole summers disappeared. I'd "live it up" on the weekends and lose the seventy percent of my life that happens between them.

Durant's line about Aristotle gets thrown around a lot, but it's worth sitting with. You don't build a life you can live with in one dramatic moment. You build it Tuesday by Tuesday. Every ordinary choice — to show up, to be present, to answer the text, to go to bed sober — is a brick. The pile of bricks eventually becomes something. Eleven years in, I can tell you: the something is quiet, and it's better than anything I was chasing.
reflection.meditation: What ordinary moment today am I actually here for?

affirmations: I am building a life one ordinary Tuesday at a time.
Today I choose presence over escape, even when the day feels small.
I am capable of showing up for the parts of my life that used to disappear.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a mind clear enough to notice the ordinary — the coffee, the quiet morning, the fact that I remember yesterday. Grateful for the friends who stuck around while I figured this out, and the ones I've met since who only know me sober. Grateful that today doesn't have to be dramatic to be good.
gratitude.closing: Just this day. That's the work.
